coaching.coaching: Plan how verification steps for each acceptance criterion will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: verification steps for each acceptance criterion

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: improve, improved

verify command `test -f $F && grep -q 'e85aaac27ad51178' $F && test $(grep -c 'arxiv.org' $F) -ge 8 && grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote
verify command `git log -1 --format=%B -- $F | grep -qi 'rebar-ticket: d39b'` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `grep -q 'Pass 1' $F && grep -q 'Pass 2' $F && grep -q 'Pass 4' $F && grep -qi 'gap analysis' $F` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `test $(grep -c '^### R' $F) -ge 5 && test $(grep -ci 'falsifiable experiment' $F) -ge 5 && test $(grep -ci 'FP assessment' $F) -ge 5` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote
verify command `grep -q 'post-claim' $F && grep -Eq '[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?%' $F` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add 'tasks-template.md' as an explicit required OSS reference in the AC1 prose and Method section (with a rationale for why that specific file must be quoted), or remove the `grep -q 'tasks-template.md'` clause from the AC1 verification command.
evidence: Proving command: `test -f $F && grep -q 'e85aaac27ad51178' $F && test $(grep -c 'arxiv.org' $F) -ge 8 && grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F`
The string 'tasks-template.md' appears nowhere else in the plan body, AC prose, Method section, or Scope/Files section — it is an undocumented prerequisite injected only into the shell check.
AC1 prose: 'A report exists at docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md with citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects, and specific quantitative data' — no mention of tasks-template.md.
findings.finding: The AC1 verification command greps for 'tasks-template.md' inside the output report, but 'tasks-template.md' is never mentioned in the plan body, steps, or AC prose as a required reference or OSS artifact. An executor following the plan faithfully could produce a report that satisfies every stated AC1 requirement (citations, quoted prompts, quantitative data, pinned hashes) yet fail this shell check because the document never happens to mention that specific filename.
scenarios: An agent researches OSS decomposition tools (SWE-agent, Moatless, Devon, etc.) and quotes their prompts, satisfying every stated AC, but never cites a file named tasks-template.md — the verification fails despite correct execution.
A future re-run of the proving command on a report that was manually verified correct returns non-zero, blocking acceptance.
findings.suggested_fix: If tasks-template.md is a known OSS prompt file the researcher must cite, add to the What/Method section: 'Quote the tasks-template.md prompt from [repo X] as a representative OSS decomposition artifact.' Then the grep check becomes justified. If it is not a deliberate requirement, delete the clause: `test -f $F && grep -q 'e85aaac27ad51178' $F && test $(grep -c 'arxiv.org' $F) -ge 8`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either state the required markdown heading format for recommendations in the What/Scope section (e.g. 'each recommendation is a ### R-prefixed third-level heading'), or replace the heading-count check with a more format-agnostic command such as `grep -ci 'recommendation' $F | awk '{if($1>=5)exit 0; exit 1}'` paired with the existing falsifiable-experiment and FP-assessment phrase counts.
evidence: AC4 prose: 'At least 5 recommendations, each with a falsifiable experiment (metric + pass/fail condition) and a false-positive assessment grounded in actual plan-review history from this repo's store.'
Proving command: `test $(grep -c '^### R' $F) -ge 5 && test $(grep -ci 'falsifiable experiment' $F) -ge 5 && test $(grep -ci 'FP assessment' $F) -ge 5`
The plan's Scope/Files and What sections give no markdown structure requirements for the recommendations section.
findings.finding: The AC4 verification command `test $(grep -c '^### R' $F) -ge 5` silently assumes all five recommendations are formatted as third-level markdown headers beginning with the letter 'R' (e.g. '### Rec 1' or '### R1'), but the plan never specifies this heading format. An executor who writes '## Recommendation 1' or '### recommendation 1' (lowercase r) would satisfy the AC4 prose ('At least 5 recommendations') yet fail the shell check.
findings.location: Verification section, AC4 proving command: `test $(grep -c '^### R' $F) -ge 5`
scenarios: Executor writes '### Recommendation 1' through '### Recommendation 5' — passes. Executor writes '## Recommendations\n### 1. ...' — fails despite five present recommendations.
The exact phrase 'FP assessment' must appear ≥5 times; if the executor writes 'false-positive assessment' inline rather than as a labeled subsection heading, the count may be <5.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Format each recommendation as a `### R<n>: <title>` third-level heading so that the verification grep anchors correctly.' Alternatively, relax the check to `grep -ci '^#\+ .*\brec' $F` with a -ge 5 threshold.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the -i flag to the 'Pass 1', 'Pass 2', and 'Pass 4' greps in the AC3 verification command: `grep -qi 'pass 1' $F && grep -qi 'pass 2' $F && grep -qi 'pass 4' $F && grep -qi 'gap analysis' $F`
evidence: 'gap analysis' uses -qi (case-insensitive) but 'Pass 1', 'Pass 2', 'Pass 4' use -q only (case-sensitive).

AC3 prose: 'The report contains a gap analysis mapping decomposition best practices to what plan-review passes 1, 2, and 4 each measure, keeping each recommendation scoped to the correct pass.' — no case requirement stated.
findings.finding: The AC3 verification uses case-sensitive grep for 'Pass 1', 'Pass 2', and 'Pass 4' (no -i flag), while AC3's prose only requires that the gap analysis be 'scoped per pass.' An executor who writes 'pass 1' (lowercase) or 'PASS 1' in section headings — both natural choices — would satisfy the intent but fail the shell check.
findings.location: Verification section, AC3 proving command: `grep -q 'Pass 1' $F && grep -q 'Pass 2' $F && grep -q 'Pass 4' $F && grep -qi 'gap analysis' $F`
scenarios: Executor writes a section heading '## Gap Analysis by pass' with subsections '### pass 1 findings' — 'gap analysis' grep passes but 'Pass 1' grep fails.
findings.suggested_fix: `grep -qi 'pass 1' $F && grep -qi 'pass 2' $F && grep -qi 'pass 4' $F && grep -qi 'gap analysis' $F`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to AC1 (and/or the Scope section) explaining what `tasks-template.md` is and why the SOTA report must reference it, so the verify command is grounded in the criterion's own text.
evidence: AC1 text: 'A report exists at docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md with citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects, and specific quantitative data, synthesizing independent research with the two Downloads reports.'
Verify AC1: `grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F`
No mention of `tasks-template.md` anywhere in What, Scope, or AC1 text.
ABSENCE: The plan body never explains what `tasks-template.md` is, why it is relevant to the SOTA report, or that it must be cited.
findings.finding: AC1's verification script checks `grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F`, but the AC1 criterion text ('citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects, and specific quantitative data') and the plan body (What items 1–2, Scope) never describe or explain why the SOTA report must reference `tasks-template.md`. An implementer reading only the criterion and plan body would not know this reference is required, yet the verify would fail without it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1 text vs. Verification – AC1 verify command
scenarios: Implementer writes a fully compliant report per AC1's text — cites papers, quotes OSS prompts, includes quantitative data — but the verify fails because they didn't happen to reference `tasks-template.md`, which was never described as a requirement.
Reviewer cannot assess whether the verify command is correct without knowing what `tasks-template.md` contains and why it belongs in this external research report.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, append: 'The report must also reference this repo's tasks-template.md as an example of an OSS decomposition artifact relevant to the gate.' Then in Scope/Files, list tasks-template.md as a read-only input. This aligns the criterion text with the verify command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an error-handling specification for the deep-research step: define a timeout bound, a minimum-coverage checkpoint (e.g., at least N distinct paper sources retrieved before synthesis begins), a partial-result recovery path (log what was collected, note the gap in the report's sources section analogously to the file-hash fallback), and at least one observable signal (log line or explicit section in the report) so a reviewer can detect a degraded execution.
evidence: Step 1 says 'Deep research on AI task decomposition: recent data-driven research (papers with data + code) and OSS community convergence' but specifies no error handling for the underlying LLM/search calls.
The Prerequisites section specifies a SHA-256 fallback for local files but there is no analogous fallback for the research phase itself.
ABSENCE: no retry, backoff, timeout, partial-result checkpoint, or observability (log/metric/alert) is named anywhere in the plan for the research execution path.
findings.finding: The deep-research step (which necessarily involves LLM calls and/or network-dependent web research) has no specified error handling: no retry policy, no timeout, no partial-result recovery path, and no observability (log/metric/alert) if the research phase fails mid-execution. If the LLM or external source is unavailable or returns a truncated result, the plan provides no instruction on whether to retry, surface a partial report, or abort — leaving the executor to improvise.
findings.location: ## What — step 1 ('Deep research on AI task decomposition'); ## Method for AC #5 (post-claim edit signal); Verification / AC1 shell checks
scenarios: LLM API rate-limit or outage mid-research produces a partial synthesis that silently passes the arxiv-citation count check in AC1 verification but misses key papers.

Network timeout during web research causes the executor to produce a report drawing only on the two Downloads files, without flagging the gap as the Prerequisites section requires for the file-hash fallback case.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the existing file-hash fallback pattern: 'If the deep-research phase yields fewer than N cited arxiv papers or encounters a retrieval failure, note the gap explicitly in the SOTA report's sources section under a "Research Coverage" heading and synthesize conservatively from the collected material only — do not extrapolate uncollected sources.' Add a verification step: `test $(grep -c 'arxiv.org' $F) -ge 8 || (echo 'WARNING: arxiv citation floor not met'; grep -qi 'research coverage' $F)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md (the Round 5 research scan + gap analysis) is a reference-to-incorporate into the new SOTA report or is being intentionally superseded; add it explicitly to the Scope read list or explain the distinction so implementers don't duplicate that synthesis from scratch.
evidence: docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md line 1: 'Plan-Review Gate — Criteria Coverage, Research Comparison, Gap Analysis & Additions (Round 5)'
docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md line 31: 'A cited scan (~30 sources) produced a 15-mode taxonomy of LLM-plan failures and mapped each to our registry'

docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md: 'These are exploratory experiment / probe artifacts, NOT automated tests and NOT production code... The production implementation will live under src/rebar/ per epic'
Plan What section: 'Synthesize with the two operator-supplied reports' — no mention of synthesizing with existing experiment gap-analysis content
Plan Scope section: lists read-only targets but does not list docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md as a source to incorporate
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new SOTA report with gap analysis mapping decomposition practices to the plan-review gate passes, but docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md already contains a '15-mode taxonomy' from a 'cited scan (~30 sources)', a per-criterion gap analysis, and criterion-to-pass mappings. The plan does not acknowledge this existing experiment document or clarify whether the new report will incorporate/supersede it or independently duplicate the same synthesis. Since docs/experiments/ is explicitly designated as 'exploratory experiment / POC artifacts, NOT production code' (per docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md), rebuilding for docs/research/ could be the intended lifecycle — but the plan's silence leaves the relationship ambiguous, risking duplicated effort.
scenarios: Implementer re-runs the 15-mode taxonomy research from scratch, producing a near-duplicate of what Round 5 already concluded, wasting LLM budget and producing a report that diverges from the grounded experiment history.
Implementer incorporates the experiment findings without attribution, making the SOTA report's provenance untraceable back to the grounded data in docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/.
findings.suggested_fix: Add docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md and docs/experiments/plan-review-decomposition-overlays-retune.md to the Scope read-only analysis list with a note: 'Incorporate and extend — these are the existing grounded experiment findings that the SOTA report formalizes into a citable committed artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific OSS project whose `tasks-template.md` is required in AC1's verification command, or replace the opaque filename grep with a check for a named, discoverable artifact (e.g. a specific GitHub repo + file).
evidence: Repo-wide search for 'tasks-template.md' returns zero hits
Plan body never names the OSS project whose tasks-template is expected
Prior cached reviews (d39b cache) independently identified this gap
The AC1 text ('citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects') does not uniquely imply this specific file
findings.finding: The AC1 verification command embeds `grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F`, asserting the SOTA report will reference a file named 'tasks-template.md'. No such file exists anywhere in the repo (search returns zero hits), and the plan body never names which OSS project's `tasks-template.md` is meant, leaving the executor unable to satisfy this check without guessing at an unspecified artifact.
scenarios: Executor completes deep research, writes valid SOTA report, commits — but the AC1 shell verification fails because no `tasks-template.md` reference was written, even though the report contains adequate OSS citations from other projects
Executor searches for 'tasks-template.md' OSS artifact and picks the wrong project, producing a citation that doesn't map to the actual decomposition SOTA
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F` with either (a) a named project reference in the plan body (e.g. 'reference SWE-bench's tasks-template.md at <URL>') so the grep is grounded, or (b) a criterion that checks for ≥N OSS project citations by URL pattern instead of a single opaque filename.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit instruction to cross-check docs/plan-review-gate.md's criteria count against the live CANONICAL_LLM frozenset in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py and criteria_routing.json before finalizing the gap analysis.
evidence: docs/plan-review-gate.md line 72: 'the 32 criteria (the F/E/G/A judgment criteria, the T1–T12 triggered overlays, COH, and ISF)'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py CANONICAL_LLM frozenset: 37 entries including T13, T14, hedge, removal-rationale which are absent from the doc's characterization
criteria_routing.json confirms T13, T14, hedge, removal-rationale are present and routed
findings.finding: The plan's gap analysis is directed at 'what plan-review passes 1, 2, and 4 each measure' using docs/plan-review-gate.md as a primary read target, but that doc asserts '32 criteria (the F/E/G/A judgment criteria, the T1–T12 triggered overlays, COH, and ISF)' while the live CANONICAL_LLM frozenset in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py contains 37 distinct criteria (F1, F4, E1–E6, G1G2, G3–G7, A1, G5, ISF, hedge, T1–T14, removal-rationale, COH). The plan names criteria_routing.json as a read target but does not instruct the executor to cross-check the doc's count against the live registry.
findings.location: Scope/Files section: 'Read-only analysis of: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ ... docs/plan-review-gate.md'
scenarios: Executor reads docs/plan-review-gate.md and produces a gap analysis based on 32 criteria, missing 5 that exist in the live gate (T13, T14, hedge, removal-rationale, plus undercounting T-overlays); recommendations about 'what the gate already covers' will be inaccurate
A recommendation proposes adding a prohibition-call-site criterion (T13) that already exists in the gate, generating a false gap finding
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/Method: 'Enumerate criteria from src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py:CANONICAL_LLM (the live authority) rather than relying on the count stated in docs/plan-review-gate.md, which may be stale.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove `grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F` from the AC1 verification command, or explain what `tasks-template.md` refers to and add it explicitly to AC1's content requirements so the report knows to reference it.
evidence: Exhaustive repo search for 'tasks-template.md' returns zero results outside of two cached plan-review sidecar findings (`.rebar/cache/plan-review/d39b-8c45-385c-418a/601eb7005d4218a9c721.json`, `73d127cdf39a236143a6.json`) that themselves flag it as a problem.
The plan body (What, AC prose, Method sections) never mentions `tasks-template.md` as a required citation or content element.
AC1 text reads 'citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects, and specific quantitative data' with no mention of `tasks-template.md`.
findings.finding: The AC1 verification command includes `grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F`, but `tasks-template.md` does not exist anywhere in the repository and is never defined or explained in the plan body. The report (`docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md`) would need to contain this exact string for the verification to pass, yet the plan gives no guidance on why or how the SOTA report should reference a file called `tasks-template.md`.
scenarios: An agent follows the plan faithfully, writes a well-formed SOTA report, runs the AC1 verification command, and it fails because the report never mentions `tasks-template.md`, with no clear corrective action since the plan never specified what it is.
The verification command is permanently unpassable unless the executor guesses that `tasks-template.md` refers to some OSS prompt-template file and fabricates a reference to it.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `grep -q 'tasks-template.md' $F` clause from the AC1 verification command (it checks for a string not defined anywhere in the plan or repo). If the intent was to verify that the report cites a specific OSS prompt-template file, name that file explicitly in the AC1 criterion text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete mechanism for accessing tickets-branch git history at execution time (e.g., specific git command, branch ref, or fallback to sidecar-based analysis via the existing `scripts/plan_review_contamination_rate.py` tooling) and add a positive rationale for why raw event-stream replay was chosen over the already-instrumented sidecar reader.
evidence: 'full history recovered from git objects of the tickets branch since the on-disk worktree is compacted' — the plan assumes git refs for the tickets branch are accessible in the snapshot, but the manifest shows the review ran against a code-branch snapshot (verified-at-sha:52d7a3dc...) and the tickets store is on a separate branch

The codebase already has scripts/plan_review_contamination_rate.py and scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py and docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py for offline sidecar analysis — purpose-built tooling that covers the reviewed-ticket segment the plan draws conclusions from
No mechanism is stated for how the agent navigates from the code snapshot to tickets branch git objects — no git command, no branch name, no fallback if the branch ref is not reachable
findings.finding: The plan's Method for AC #5 specifies 'full history recovered from git objects of the tickets branch' but provides no positive rationale for this approach over the already-instrumented sidecar reader path (sidecar.py + scripts/plan_review_contamination_rate.py / calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py), and more critically, does not specify the mechanism by which the executing agent accesses the tickets branch's git history from within a code-branch snapshot — this is a hidden accessibility assumption that may be unresolvable at execution time.
scenarios: Agent executes in the code-branch snapshot and cannot resolve refs/heads/tickets (or equivalent) — the entire AC5 methodology fails silently or with an opaque git error
The tickets branch IS reachable but the agent uses git log / git cat-file without the correct object paths — partial history recovered with no signal to the plan that it's incomplete
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Method for AC #5 section: (a) the explicit git command to recover tickets-branch objects (e.g., `git log refs/heads/tickets` or `git cat-file --batch` with the correct ref), (b) a fallback: 'if the tickets branch is not reachable from this snapshot, use the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar reader (sidecar.py:prior_concerns / latest_review_result) cross-referenced against store updated_at timestamps for the reviewed-ticket segment,' and (c) a one-sentence rationale for why full event stream replay (rather than sidecar + updated_at) was chosen for the unreviewed-ticket baseline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for why a new standalone SOTA file is produced rather than extending docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md, and state how the new report should relate to (cite, extend, or supersede) the existing gap analysis.
evidence: docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md exists and contains: '~30 sources produced a 15-mode taxonomy of LLM-plan failures and mapped each to our registry' — directly relevant to What item 1
The plan's What item 1 says 'Deep research on AI task decomposition: recent data-driven research (papers with data + code)' with no mention of this existing prior research artifact
No positive rationale is stated for why the approach produces a new file (docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md) rather than extending or updating the existing experiments document
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for why the SOTA synthesis approach (live deep research + two operator reports → markdown file) is the chosen method rather than building on the repo's own existing gap analysis in docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md, which already contains a 15-mode failure taxonomy, 30-source research scan, and gap mapping — the plan's approach may duplicate rather than extend that prior work.
scenarios: The executing agent produces a SOTA report that substantially duplicates the existing docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md without realizing it, wasting effort and producing a lower-quality synthesis than extending the existing work would yield

The agent correctly identifies the prior work, but without guidance in the plan on how to relate to it, produces recommendations that conflict with or regress the already-validated decisions in that document

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference `docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md` explicitly in the plan (What §2 or Scope), state what the new report adds beyond it (e.g. updated post-G6/T10-implementation signal, operator-supplied report synthesis, post-claim edit quantification), and declare whether the new file supersedes, extends, or complements the existing one.
evidence: Plan What §1: 'Deep research on AI task decomposition: recent data-driven research (papers with data + code) and OSS community convergence'
Plan What §3: 'Map the report's practices onto what plan review measures today (criteria registry, DET floor, LLM criteria)'

docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md §Q2: 'A cited scan (~30 sources) produced a 15-mode taxonomy of LLM-plan failures and mapped each to our registry' referencing arXiv:2604.11978, arXiv:2509.25370
docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md §Q7: DSO approach-decision-maker/proposer analysis, adopted into G6
docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md result line: '4 new criteria (G6, T10, T11, T12) + 8 roll-in extensions'
ABSENCE: the plan contains no reference to docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md and provides no explanation of what the new report contributes beyond that document
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new SOTA synthesis report (docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md) covering 2025-2026 task-decomposition research, per-pass gap analysis, and ≥5 recommendations — but docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md already contains substantially the same outputs: a cited 30-source research scan with a 15-mode failure taxonomy, a DSO decider comparison, per-criterion gap mapping, and recommendations that were acted on (G6, T10, T11, T12). The plan does not cite this prior artifact, acknowledge what it adds incrementally, or justify producing a new file rather than graduating the existing document.
scenarios: The executing agent produces a 15-mode failure taxonomy that duplicates (or silently contradicts) the one in the experiments doc, creating two divergent sources of truth with no reconciliation path.
The SOTA report's gap analysis re-discovers G6/T10/T11/T12 as 'gaps' without knowing they were already added, making the recommendations stale on arrival.
A future reader of both docs cannot determine which is authoritative, undermining the committed report's stated purpose as a durable 'SOTA' reference.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to What §2: 'The new report builds on and supersedes [or: complements] docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-coverage-and-gaps.md (which contains the prior 30-source scan and 15-mode taxonomy); it advances that baseline by [specific delta, e.g. post-implementation signal, AC5 post-claim edit quantification, operator-supplied report synthesis, updated recommendations scoped to criteria added since Round 5].' Cross-link both documents so there is one canonical chain of custody.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific tool or capability that enables live 2025-2026 paper retrieval and OSS source access (e.g. web-search MCP, named corpus), and add a web-access-absent fallback that honestly constrains AC #1 scope (e.g. 'if no live web access: synthesize from training knowledge + operator reports; cite only sources verifiable from training data; note the constraint explicitly in the report's sources section').
evidence: AC #1: 'A report ... with citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects, and specific quantitative data'
Verification AC1: 'test $(grep -c \'arxiv.org\' $F) -ge 8'
Prerequisites fallback: 'synthesize from the deep-research findings alone — do not fabricate content from a missing/changed file'
ABSENCE: no web-search tool, MCP server, or named corpus is declared anywhere in the plan to enable retrieval of 2025-2026 arxiv papers or live OSS prompts
findings.finding: AC #1 requires 'citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects, and specific quantitative data,' and the Verification step checks for ≥8 arxiv.org links; What §1 calls for 'recent data-driven research (papers with data + code).' These collectively presuppose live internet/web-search access to retrieve 2025-2026 papers and OSS content, but the plan never names what tool or capability provides this access. The only stated fallback (Prerequisites section) addresses operator-file absence, not web-access absence — leaving AC #1 satisfiable only under an unstated, unresolved precondition.
findings.location: What §1 / Prerequisites / AC #1 / Verification (AC1 shell command)
scenarios: The agent lacks live web-search access: it synthesizes from training knowledge, citations are approximate or unverifiable, quoted OSS prompts may be stale — AC #1 and its Verification grep cannot be honestly satisfied.
The agent has web-search access via an undeclared tool: a reviewer reading the plan cannot assess whether the cited content is reproducibly reachable or whether the approach is sound.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the internet-access capability gap before committing to this plan: either (a) specify a web-search MCP server configuration (e.g. `REBAR_LLM_MCP_SERVERS`) that provides HTTP/search capability and confirm it is available in the execution environment, (b) scope the 'deep research' to synthesize from the two operator-supplied Downloads files plus training-data knowledge (and remove the ≥8 arXiv citation shell check from AC1 verification), or (c) route to a SPIKE to determine what research tooling is available before committing the full plan.
evidence: Plan 'What §1': 'Deep research on AI task decomposition: recent data-driven research (papers with data + code) and OSS community convergence observed in skills/prompts that decompose software-engineering tasks'
AC1: 'A report exists at docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md with citations, quoted code/prompts from OSS projects, and specific quantitative data'
Verification AC1: 'test $(grep -c 'arxiv.org' $F) -ge 8' — requires ≥8 arXiv citations in the output file

src/rebar/llm/runner.py lines 239-248: tools = pai_tools.filesystem_tools(cfg.repo_path) + pai_tools.grounding_tools(cfg.repo_path) + pai_tools.rebar_tools(...) — all repo-scoped; no web-search or HTTP-fetch tool
No MCP server providing web-search is named or configured in the plan
Evidence class: Contradicted — the environment's tool surface does not include internet access
findings.finding: The plan's core deliverable requires internet/web-search access ('Deep research on AI task decomposition: recent data-driven research (papers with data + code) and OSS community convergence'), but the agent's verified tool surface is entirely repo-scoped (read_file, list_directory, search_files, resolve_symbol, show_ticket — no HTTP-fetch or search API tool). AC1 verification further requires ≥8 arXiv citations and quoted OSS code/prompts, which cannot be satisfied without live internet access. This is a critical capability gap with no SPIKE proposed.
findings.location: What §1 / Acceptance Criteria AC1 / Verification AC1 proving command
scenarios: Agent begins executing What §1 and cannot fetch arXiv papers or OSS repository content; either the deliverable cannot be produced or is fabricated from training-data knowledge without verified current citations — both violate AC1.
AC1 verification `grep -c 'arxiv.org' $F` returns < 8 because the agent could not actually retrieve papers, causing the verification to fail even if the plan was otherwise followed faithfully.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Prerequisites section, add: 'A web-search MCP server (e.g. Brave Search, Perplexity, or equivalent) is configured via REBAR_LLM_MCP_SERVERS and verified reachable before execution.' If no such server is available, revise What §1 to 'Synthesize from the two operator-supplied reports plus prior training knowledge' and replace the `grep -c 'arxiv.org' -ge 8` check with a softer citation-count assertion (e.g. ≥3) that the two Downloads files and training knowledge can realistically satisfy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) specify that the two Downloads files must be copied into the repo worktree (e.g. `docs/research/inputs/`) before execution begins and update the Prerequisites paths accordingly, or (b) explicitly state that these files are read via an out-of-band mechanism (e.g. pasted into the ticket description or provided as context) rather than via the agent's file tools — and confirm this is compatible with the SHA-256 verification step.
evidence: Prerequisites: 'The two operator-supplied input reports have been verified present and read at execution time: ~/Downloads/ai-task-decomposition-report.md … ~/Downloads/AI Task Decomposition Best Practices.md'
src/rebar/llm/runner.py line 240: 'pai_tools.filesystem_tools(cfg.repo_path)' — tools are scoped to cfg.repo_path, a git snapshot root, not the user's home directory
src/rebar/llm/fs_tools.py: filesystem tools operate under a repo_root mount; paths outside it are inaccessible
Evidence class: Contradicted — ~/Downloads/ is outside cfg.repo_path and the agent cannot read it with the standard tool surface
findings.finding: The plan reads two operator-supplied input files at `~/Downloads/ai-task-decomposition-report.md` and `~/Downloads/AI Task Decomposition Best Practices.md` — paths outside the repo root. The agent's read_file tool is scoped to cfg.repo_path (the attested code snapshot root), so these paths are unreachable via the standard tool surface. No auth or path-override precondition is stated.
scenarios: Agent calls read_file('~/Downloads/ai-task-decomposition-report.md') and receives a 'path outside repo root' error or silent failure; the Prerequisites SHA-256 verification step cannot execute, triggering the fallback clause — but the fallback still requires the agent to have attempted the read.

The plan's Fallback clause ('if either file is absent or its hash differs … note the gap explicitly') depends on the agent being able to attempt the read; if the tool surface itself prevents the read, the fallback is unreachable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a setup step: 'Before running the agent, copy both operator-supplied reports into `docs/research/inputs/` in the repo worktree; update the Prerequisites paths to `docs/research/inputs/ai-task-decomposition-report.md` and `docs/research/inputs/AI Task Decomposition Best Practices.md`.' Then the agent's read_file tool can access them and the SHA-256 check can run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the cross-reference to 'the attestation material-fingerprint definition' in the AC #5 materiality filter with a direct rule: enumerate the specific `data.fields` key(s) in an EDIT event that qualify as material (e.g., `description` — which subsumes ACs — and `file_impact`), and clarify that 'AC' is part of the `description` field, not a separate key.
evidence: Plan: 'only description/AC/file-impact changes count (matching the attestation material-fingerprint definition)'
pass1.py:551-562: material_fingerprint hashes {ticket_id, description, file_impact, children} — no 'AC' field exists separately; description contains ACs
The cross-reference points to a ticket-content hash, not an event-field classification decision rule
The plan says 'AC' is material but the fingerprint has no separate AC field — the executor must infer that 'AC' is part of 'description', which is not stated
findings.finding: The 'Method for AC #5' materiality filter references 'the attestation material-fingerprint definition' as its authority for which EDIT-event fields count as material, but `material_fingerprint()` (pass1.py:551–562) is a ticket-content hash (`description + file_impact + children`) — it is NOT a protocol for classifying EDIT-event fields. The actual rule an executor needs (filter EDIT events whose `data.fields` keys intersect `{description, file_impact}`) is never stated, and the cross-reference misleads: `file_impact` changes appear in the hash but 'AC' is cited in the prose while absent from the hash basis.
scenarios: Executor implements the filter by reading material_fingerprint() source and discovers it hashes whole description (which includes ACs) — not individual EDIT-event field names — and cannot directly apply it to classify individual EDIT events
Executor misreads 'AC' as a distinct field and writes a filter that never fires because no separate AC key exists in the event store
findings.suggested_fix: State the filter rule explicitly: 'An EDIT event is material if its `data.fields` contains any of: `description`, `file_impact`. (`description` subsumes AC content; there is no separate AC key in the event store.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact denominator construction order for AC #5: state whether compacted-ticket exclusion is applied before or after review-result segmentation, and explicitly define what denominator is used for the missed-finding rate (e.g., 'reviewed, non-compacted tickets only').
evidence: Plan: 'Compaction (SNAPSHOT events) may hide early history for some tickets; those are excluded from the denominator'
Plan: 'only draws missed-finding conclusions from the reviewed segment'

No statement of how to handle tickets that are BOTH reviewed (REVIEW_RESULT exists) AND compacted (early history hidden) — should they appear in the reviewed segment's denominator or be excluded entirely?
findings.finding: The AC #5 analysis protocol specifies two overlapping denominator filters — (1) exclude SNAPSHOT-compacted tickets and (2) segment by prior REVIEW_RESULT existence — but never specifies the combination order or how to handle tickets that satisfy both conditions (i.e., reviewed tickets whose early history is compacted). This is an ambiguous counter placement: the denominator of the missed-finding rate is undefined for this intersection.
findings.location: Method for AC #5 — 'Confound acknowledged' paragraph and 'Compaction' note
scenarios: Executor A excludes compacted tickets first (reducing both the reviewed and unreviewed segments), then reports the rate only over reviewed non-compacted tickets
Executor B segments first, then excludes compacted tickets only from the denominator of the missed-finding conclusion, but includes them in the total count — producing a different rate
The two approaches yield different quantitative results, undermining the reproducibility of the AC #5 signal cited in recommendations

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rule for the git-history recovery step in AC #5: specify what to do when git object recovery fails for a ticket (e.g., 'exclude that ticket from both numerator and denominator; report the count of excluded tickets in the SOTA report's sources section').
evidence: Plan: 'full history recovered from git objects of the tickets branch since the on-disk worktree is compacted'

Plan's Prerequisites section: 'if either file is absent or its hash differs … note the gap explicitly in the SOTA report's sources section and synthesize from the deep-research findings alone' — this pattern is NOT replicated for the git recovery step
No analogous 'if git recovery fails, exclude or note' instruction exists for AC #5
findings.finding: The AC #5 data-collection step specifies that full ticket event history will be recovered from git objects ('full history recovered from git objects of the tickets branch since the on-disk worktree is compacted') but provides no fallback when this git recovery fails (e.g., shallow clone, missing/packed objects, detached HEAD with no tickets branch). The Prerequisites fallback pattern (for missing input files) is not applied here.
findings.location: Method for AC #5 — 'Data: replay each work ticket's event stream' and Scope note about git objects
scenarios: Git objects are unavailable (shallow clone, missing pack files) — executor cannot recover early event history for some or all tickets; the denominator silently shrinks or the method silently fails
Partial git recovery succeeds for some tickets and fails for others — executor has no guidance on whether to include or exclude partial recoveries from the count

findings.finding: The Verification section provides only two commands — a file-existence check and a git-trailer grep — which together prove only AC#2 (file committed with trailer). ACs #1, #3, #4, and #5 — which require specific content (citations, quoted OSS prompts/code, quantitative data, a gap-analysis section covering passes 1/2/4, ≥5 recommendations each with falsifiable experiment + FP assessment, and the post-claim edit signal quantified and cited) — have no proving command that inspects the file's structure or content.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Verification:
```
# AC#1: at least one citation bracket and one code-block quote
grep -qE '\[.+\]\(.+\)' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md
grep -q '```' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md
# AC#3: gap-analysis section heading exists and names all three passes
grep -qi 'pass 1' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md && grep -qi 'pass 2' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md && grep -qi 'pass 4' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md
# AC#4: at least 5 recommendation entries (e.g. numbered headings or list items labeled 'Recommendation')
python3 -c "import re,sys; t=open('docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md').read(); assert len(re.findall(r'(?im)^#+\s+recommendation', t)) >= 5 or len(re.findall(r'(?im)^\d+\.\s+\*\*recommendation', t)) >= 5"
# AC#5: post-claim edit signal quantified and cited
grep -qi 'post.claim\|in_progress.*edit\|edit.*in_progress' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md
```

findings.finding: ACs #3, #4, and #5 each specify substantive content requirements (gap analysis with per-pass scoping; ≥5 recommendations each with a falsifiable experiment and a false-positive assessment grounded in repo history; post-claim edit signal quantified and cited in a recommendation), yet the Verification section provides no check for any of these — only `test -f` (file existence, covering AC #1 partially) and `grep -i rebar-ticket` (covering AC #2). An implementer or reviewer cannot mechanically confirm these three ACs were met.
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend the Verification section with at minimum:
```
# AC #3 — gap analysis covers all three passes
grep -E 'Pass (1|2|4|pass-1|pass-2|pass-4)' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md | wc -l  # expect ≥ 3

# AC #4 — at least 5 recommendations present
grep -c '## Recommendation' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md  # expect ≥ 5

# AC #5 — post-claim signal cited
grep -i 'post.claim\|in_progress.*edit\|edit.*in_progress' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md
```
Adjust grep patterns to match the document's actual heading/terminology conventions.

findings.finding: Three of the five Acceptance Criteria have no proving command in the Verification section: (AC3) 'gap analysis mapping best practices to passes 1, 2, and 4'; (AC4) '≥5 recommendations each with a falsifiable experiment and false-positive assessment'; (AC5) 'post-claim ticket-edit signal quantified and cited.' The two existing verification commands only prove file existence and the git trailer, leaving content-level claims unverifiable by an automated check.
findings.location: Verification section / Acceptance Criteria AC3, AC4, AC5
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to Verification:
```
# AC3: gap analysis covers all three passes
grep -iE 'pass (1|2|4|one|two|four)' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md | wc -l  # expect ≥3 distinct pass references

# AC4: at least 5 recommendations each with experiment + FP assessment
python3 - <<'EOF'
import re, sys
body = open('docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md').read()
recs = re.findall(r'(?im)^#{2,3}\s+recommendation', body)
assert len(recs) >= 5, f'only {len(recs)} recommendations found'
for r in recs:
    assert 'experiment' in body[body.index(r):body.index(r)+2000].lower(), f'no experiment near {r}'
print('AC4 PASS')
EOF

# AC5: ticket-edit quantification present
grep -iE 'in.progress|ticket.edit|edited after' docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md
```

findings.finding: AC5 requires quantifying 'the post-claim ticket-edit signal from store history (events on the tickets branch)' and citing it in at least one recommendation. The tickets branch is a git orphan branch mounted as a worktree at .tickets-tracker/ (git-ignored state directory). The plan names no mechanism for the agent to read this history — no script invocation, no git command, no API, and no statement that .tickets-tracker/ will be accessible. The agent's filesystem deny-list typically excludes state paths; no auth/access precondition is stated.

The plan states the two operator-supplied reports 'have been verified present and read at execution time' at paths ~/Downloads/ai-task-decomposition-report.md and ~/Downloads/AI Task Decomposition Best Practices.md. Both paths are outside the repo root. The agent's filesystem_tools() in src/rebar/llm/pai_tools.py enforces a realpath-based confinement: _safe_path() rejects paths that resolve outside the repo root, and ~/Downloads/ will never resolve inside the repo root. No mechanism is stated for reading these files through the agent's constrained tool surface. The plan's fallback clause ('if either file is absent or its hash differs... synthesize from the deep-research findings alone') only covers the file-absence/hash-mismatch scenario — it does not address the structural inability to read ~/Downloads/ paths through the available tools.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a setup step: 'Before running the agent, copy ~/Downloads/ai-task-decomposition-report.md and ~/Downloads/AI Task Decomposition Best Practices.md into docs/research/input/ (gitignored), and update the Prerequisites to reference those repo-relative paths.' The agent can then read them via its existing filesystem_tools() surface.

findings.finding: The plan names two external input files — '~/Downloads/ai-task-decomposition-report.md' and 'AI Task Decomposition Best Practices.md' — as source material to synthesize into the deliverable. The agent's filesystem tools enforce repo-root confinement via _safe_path(), which raises ValueError for any path that escapes the repo root (e.g. ~/Downloads/). These files are structurally inaccessible to the agent as specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a setup step: 'Copy ~/Downloads/ai-task-decomposition-report.md and ~/Downloads/AI Task Decomposition Best Practices.md into docs/research/inputs/ before claiming this ticket.' Then reference them by their in-repo paths.

findings.finding: The Scope/Files section lists `src/rebar/plan_review/` as a read-only analysis target, but this path does not exist in the repository. The actual plan-review implementation lives at `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`. An agent reading the plan would either silently fail to find files at the stated path or skip the analysis entirely, invisibly omitting the core implementation from the SOTA report's gap analysis.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `src/rebar/plan_review/` with `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` in the Scope / Files section.

findings.finding: The Scope/Files section lists `.rebar/prompts/` as a read-only analysis target, but this directory does not exist in this repository. The `.rebar/` directory contains only `cache/` and `trusted_environments.yaml`. `.rebar/prompts/` is a project-override mechanism that is optionally present; in this repo it is absent, so an agent following the plan would read an empty or nonexistent path.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the scope bullet to: '`.rebar/prompts/` (project prompt overrides — absent in this repo, no overrides active)'.

findings.finding: The Scope/Files section lists `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` as a read-only analysis target, but this project-overlay file does not exist in this repository (`.rebar/` only contains `cache/` and `trusted_environments.yaml`). The actual production criteria routing file is at `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json`. An agent reading the stated path finds nothing, potentially leading it to conclude that no custom routing is applied when in fact the production routing is the packaged one.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` in the Scope with `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json` (the committed production routing), and add a note that `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` is an optional project overlay (absent here).

findings.finding: The Scope section names 'src/rebar/plan_review/' as a read-only analysis target, but this path does not exist in the repository. The actual plan-review implementation lives at 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/'.
findings.location: Scope / Files section: 'Read-only analysis of: src/rebar/llm/ (plan-review passes and criteria registry), src/rebar/plan_review/, ...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `src/rebar/plan_review/` with `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` in the Scope / Files section.

findings.finding: The 'Method for AC #5' materiality filter references 'the attestation material-fingerprint definition' as its authority for which field changes count, but this definition is never stated inline in the plan and no pointer to its location is provided. An executor running the event-stream analysis cannot determine the exact field set (description / AC / file_impact) without searching the codebase — the decision rule is not co-located with the schema it depends on.
findings.location: Method for AC #5 (post-claim edit signal) — 'Materiality filter' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical or footnote immediately after the materiality filter sentence: '(fields: description, file_impact, children — as defined in pass1.py::material_fingerprint; tag, assignee, comment, and link-only changes are excluded)'.

findings.finding: The 'Method for AC #5' section requires replaying event streams from '.tickets-tracker/<id>/' but specifies no fallback if the tickets store is inaccessible (e.g., the tickets branch worktree has not been checked out in the execution environment). Only the SNAPSHOT-compaction data-loss case has an explicit exclusion rule; a fully unavailable store has no specified response.
findings.location: Method for AC #5 (post-claim edit signal) — 'Data' and 'Confound acknowledged' paragraphs

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
